critic_raw:
This experiment compares a multi-spectral CNN (10 bands) versus an RGB-only CNN for predicting algal biomass from summer satellite imagery.

The key confound in remote sensing algal detection is sun-glint and atmospheric scattering, which affect different spectral bands differently. Atmospheric correction is described as a preprocessing step 'fit on the training set only.' However, atmospheric correction is a physical model (e.g., 6SV or MODTRAN) parameterized by atmospheric conditions, sun-satellite geometry, and surface reflectance, not a statistical transformation that can be 'fit' on training data. Applying training-set atmospheric correction to validation/test images would mean using training-set atmospheric conditions to correct images acquired under potentially different atmospheric conditions. This is scientifically incorrect; atmospheric correction must be applied per-image using the conditions at acquisition time. Describing it as a trainable transform suggests a fundamental misunderstanding of the preprocessing pipeline.

The in-situ measurements are collected 'within ±2 days of image acquisition.' Algal bloom dynamics can change dramatically within 2 days: chlorophyll-a concentrations can vary by factors of 2–5 under favorable growth conditions. A 2-day mismatch between ground truth and image means the label may not correspond to the image state. The RGB-only baseline exploits fewer spectral bands but is subject to the same temporal mismatch. If bloom dynamics are fast, both models will have high label noise from temporal misalignment, and the comparison may reflect noise floor rather than spectral discriminability.

The experiment restricts to summer months (June–August) only. Multi-spectral indices for algae (e.g., NDCI, cyanobacteria index) have known seasonal relationships with chlorophyll fluorescence that are optimized for summer growth conditions. The reported performance is thus constrained to a narrow seasonal window, but the experimental design does not acknowledge whether the model would fail in spring or fall when water optical properties differ. The claim that multi-spectral information outperforms RGB is only testable within the summer seasonal window.

Spatial patches of 64×128 pixels centered on the sampling location are described. For freshwater lakes, the sampling location is typically a buoy or fixed station at the lake center. If the CNN patch is centered on the same fixed location across years, the model learns spectral signatures of that specific location rather than generalizing to the spatial heterogeneity of the lake. This is particularly problematic for per-lake evaluation: a model that memorizes the spectral signature of a fixed sampling station will have low per-lake MAE without generalizing to the full lake surface.
all_issues_raised: Atmospheric correction is described as a preprocessing step 'fit on training data.' Atmospheric correction is a physics-based per-image transform parameterized by acquisition-time atmospheric conditions, not a statistical transform that can be trained on historical data and applied to new images under different conditions.

Ground-truth biomass measurements are collected within ±2 days of image acquisition. Algal bloom dynamics can shift by 2–5x within 48 hours under favorable conditions, meaning the label may not represent the bloom state visible in the image, introducing systematic label noise.

The experiment is restricted to summer months only. Multi-spectral spectral indices optimized for algal detection are tuned to summer fluorescence conditions; claims about multi-spectral advantage do not generalize to other seasons with different water optical properties.

Spatial patches centered on fixed sampling stations across years cause the model to learn the spectral signature of a fixed geographic location rather than generalizing spatially across the lake surface, inflating per-lake MAE performance estimates.
all_issues_adjudicated: Atmospheric correction is described as a preprocessing step 'fit on training data.' Atmospheric correction is a physics-based per-image transform parameterized by acquisition-time atmospheric conditions, not a statistical transform that can be trained on historical data and applied to new images under different conditions.

Ground-truth biomass measurements are collected within ±2 days of image acquisition. Algal bloom dynamics can shift by 2–5x within 48 hours under favorable conditions, meaning the label may not represent the bloom state visible in the image, introducing systematic label noise.

The experiment is restricted to summer months only. Multi-spectral spectral indices optimized for algal detection are tuned to summer fluorescence conditions; claims about multi-spectral advantage do not generalize to other seasons with different water optical properties.

Spatial patches centered on fixed sampling stations across years cause the model to learn the spectral signature of a fixed geographic location rather than generalizing spatially across the lake surface, inflating per-lake MAE performance estimates.
